May 13, 1976

Mr. Don A. Allen, Sr.
Executive Secretary-Treasurer
Association of Former California Legislators
837 Commons Drive
Sacramento CA 95825

Dear Mr. Allen:

Thank you for your letter of May 3, 1976, seeking
advice with respect to the homecoming event of the
Association of Former California Legislators. In a
telephone conversation explaining your letter, you
informed me that attending among the former legislators who
are eligible to attend the event are individuals who are
presently elected state officials (for example, Lt. Gov.
Dymally, Sec'y. of State Eu, Treasurer Unruh).

The prohibition against gifts from lobbyists applies
to gifts to elected state officials. Accordingly, although
the above mentioned individuals are no longer legislators,
they nevertheless still are individuals to whom lobbyists
cannot make gifts of $10 or more in a calendar month. To
avoid any problems under the Political Reform Act, you
should ask that these individuals pay for their own meals
and drinks instead of having a lobbyist pay for these items.
Of course, with respect to former legislators who are not
state candidates, elected state officials, legislative
officials or agency officials, no problem is created by
having a lobbyist pay for the costs of the event.

If you have any further questions, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

Very truly yours,

Kenneth Finney
Chief
Legal Division

Association of Former California Legislators

B37 COMMONS DRIVE, SACRAMENTO, CA. 95825
Phone 916-927-5115

May 3, 1976

LETTER FOR
R/E CONSIDERATION
5/7/76

Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman
Fair Political Practice Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sir:

In accord with telephone inquiry made to Executive Director
Michael Bennett, and his advice, I respectfully submit the following
question.

Is there any prohibition or restriction on a lobbyist making a
contribution toward the Homecoming of Former
California Legislators? The event will take place in Sacramento
June 10th and 11th, 1976.

Our association is a historian, social and commerative group of
former California legislators. We are non-partisan - no longer political
in the sense that we do not endorse candidates or issues. We have never
caused to be introduced or advocated bills or measures to the legislature,
nor will we ever do so. I enclose two of our quarterly bulletins so that
you may judge the function of our association.

We ask this question for clarification because prior to Proposition
9, many members of the Third House wanted to participate with us in the
renewal of old friendships. To that end, they would defray the costs
of some meals and cocktails at an overall equally shared average of
$600.00 per Homecoming.

Many of the Third House members would like to continue this tra-
dition. We, and they, want to operate within the law. Thank you for
your consideration and advice.

Semper Vaya con Dios,

Don A. Allen, Sr.
Executive Secretary-Treasurer

Association of Former California Legislators

April 26, 1976                837 COMMONS DRIVE - SACRAMENTO, CALIF. 95825 - (916) 927-5115

TRUSTEES
Hon. Charles W. Jones, Chairman; Hon. Lery E. Lyon, Jr., Hon. Thomas A. Maloney, Hon. William F. Merah, Hon. Harold J. Powers, Hon. George W. Rochester, Hon. Kenneth A.
Cory, Jr., Hon. Don A. Allen, S., Secretary-Treasurer CHAPLAINS: Hon. Jesse R. Kellems, D.D., Hon. F. Douglas Ferris, D.D., Hon. Reuben Breiwa,
D.D.

FROM THE SECRETARY TREASURER

This year's Homecoming should be a
most dramatic, important and semi-
mental journey for all, because it will be
the last time for the next five years any-
one, other than construction workers,
will be allowed to enter the Assembly or
Senate Chambers in the Old Capitol
Building.

Knowing this visit will mean so
much to those who have served in these
historic halls, Assembly Speaker Leo T.
McCarthy and Senate President Pro Tem-
pore James R. Mills, have designated
special representatives to assure all our
cooperation to make this Homecoming a
most pleasant visit of all former members.

Honorable John C. Williamson, one
of our members and now serving as Chief
Executive Officer of the Senate Rules
Committee, will represent Senator Mills;
Speaker McCarthy is represented by his
Special Assistant, Carlos Rodrigues.

The Senator Hotel will again be our
Headquarters. The hotel has undergone
considerable renovation in the past year,
and more is in process. It is most difficult
to find accommodations close to the Capi-
tol Building because of the growing use
of the new nearby convention center.

Due to this competition, exact reser-
vations must be fulfilled. This causes
considerable negotiations and it does
create mutual responsibility.

It now requires specific guarantees.
Your prompt reservations are a must; as
far as dinner and luncheon are con-
cerned, I am in the process of getting the
lunch and noon cocktails honed. How-
ever, Proposition Nine has created some
problems. Return Enclosed Post Card
Immediately.

WE WELCOME NEW MEMBERS

Senator Donald L. Grunsky, is now
a charter member. He first served in the
Assembly from 1947 to 1952; elected to
Senate in 1962, he has decided to retire at
the end of this term; he will have served
30 Legislative years.

Joining the Associate Members, is
Mrs. Ovidia A. Bush, wife of the late

Superior Court Judge, David F. Bush, re-
tired. One of our charter members, Judge
Bush served in the California Senate 1931
to 1934.

JUDGE BECK HONORED

Judge Julian Beck and Professor
Lynwood Wren were honored for out-
standing service to their communities,
and long dedicated service to their profes-
sions by the Association of California
State University Professors.

Dr. Del Stelck, president of the
organization's CSUN chapter, presented
the awards.

Judge Beck was honored by the
ACSUP for his contributions to the uni-
versity as well as his community work. He
began his career in 1931, when he began
teaching at San Fernando High School.
He began practicing law in 1935.

Beck was elected to the California
State Assembly in 1943. Ten years later,
he was appointed to the Municipal Court
by Earl Warren. In 1959 Beck became a
Superior Court Justice, a post he held
until his retirement last May.

While in the state legislature, Beck
brought the San Fernando Valley its first
Municipal and Superior courts, the San
Fernando Valley Fair and is credited with
having persuaded the legislature to
change the proposed site for the building
of a new state college, (originally to be an
extension of Cal State L.A.), to the San
Fernando Valley. That college is today
known as CSUN.

The judge was also responsible for
swinging the Senate in favor of a bill
which proposed creation of the UCLA
Law school.

The judge serves as chairman of the
College Advisory Board.

JUDGE WESTOVER RETIRES

U.S. District Court Judge Harry C.
Westover, 81, has retired after 26 years of
service. He was appointed to the federal
bench by President Harry S. Truman in
1949. Judge Westover served as Senator
from Orange County 1937 to 1940.

THE HOMECOMING
JUNE 10, 1976
JUNE 11, 1976

BICENTENNIAL NOTES
It isn't publicized too much, but the
United States Declaration of Independ-
ence was not adopted on the fourth of
July, 1776 . . . even though that doc-
ument is so dated.

The colonists appointed a com-
mittee to draft the declaration which
would separate them from England.
Named on the committee were Benjamin
Franklin, Thomas Jefferson, John Adams,
Roger Sherman and Robert R. Livingston. The committee entrusted the actual
drafting of the document to young
Thomas Jefferson.

It was on July 2 that a vote was
taken among the delegates from the 13
colonies. Ten voted in favor of adoption,
New York did not vote and Pennsylvania
and Delaware cast divided ballots in the
negative. Thus, the resolution was
adopted on July 2, but it was on July
fourth that Jefferson's "Declaration" was
adopted.

New York voted in favor of the De-
claration on July 9th but it wasn't
announced to Congress until the 15th.
Then on the 19th, it was declared to be
unanimous and ordered engrossed under
the heading of "In Congress, July 4,
1776."

This was truly a divided nation in
those days. Only about one-third of the
colonists wanted to form a new republic,
about a third wanted to remain with the
British crown and the other third cared
little one way or another.

Fifty-six men placed their signatures
on that historic document and by so doing, confirmed that they were committing
an act of treason.

To indicate the extent of their sacrifice one man had 11 children and all 11
lost their life in the years to come because of that signature.

Charles Carroll of Carrollton, Maryland was perhaps, the wealthiest of all the
signers . . . it is estimated that he was
worth about a million dollars; a princely
sum in those days. He said he would rather
than likely lose everything he had . . .
and he did. He was a pauper when he
died.

There are many similar stories among
the 56 names on that revered document
we call the "Declaration of Independence".

Freedom is bought by determination, sacrifice and it requires constant
vigilance to keep the flame of liberty
always ablaze in the hearts of free
peoples.

inevitable, "To what do you attribute

"Not sure yet," the old-timer replied, eyes a-twinkle, "I'm still negotiating with a mattress company and two

Spanish-Language Ballot a Step Backward

Those voting in recent local and special elections have been presented with
material and ballots printed in Spanish.
This is the result
of new state laws
enacted last year
and enforced in this
election material
to be printed
in that language
and any other for
which the county
clerk may determine a need. This
can well include
Chinese, Japanese, German and Italian as well as others where a substantial number of immigrants have formed a community.

HAD THE LAW BEEN on the books a
few years ago there might have been
some grumbling about ballots in "other"
because of the influx of migrants from
that southern dust bowl area.
Secretary of State March Fong Eu has
estimated that the California law, along
with a newly passed congressional act,

will cost the state $40 million additional in
the elections over next year. They have
underestimated the total cost to the citizens for the displaced that California
alone will require one-third of the total
unreimbursed newsprint supply in the
United States and Canada for next year's
ballots.

There is no demand for equal and fair treatment, many have asked for ballots in various
languages and other for
which the county
clerk may determine a need. This
can well include
Chinese, Japanese, German and Italian as well as others where a substantial number of immigrants have formed a community.

ALSO HAS an environmental aspect
for Secretary Eu said the paper needed is
equivalent to 10,000 full-grown fir trees.

There can be little quarrel with the
idea that every citizen should be afforded
an opportunity to exercise their
voting rights and to make that duty easy
to perform.

But is it in the best interests of the
nation to coddle its citizens to the extent
that we are not spurred into learning the official language of the country? And is it in
the interest of the state to permit the voting of persons who can't read English

well enough to mark their ballots?

As every effort is made at the polls not
to arrive at merely by reading the explanations of
measures and candidates furnished by the
county clerk in the sample ballot material. If that were so there would be no need
for the campaigning dialogues and materials or the newspaper coverage of the
issues.

JUDGMENTS ARE FORMED by the
voters based upon what they can learn
from platform discussions and printed
materials and from the media. The overwhelming bulk of this is in English for
the not astonishing reason that it is the
language of the country.

The question then is are those who will
now vote, only because the ballot is printed in a language they understand, sufficiently informed to make intelligent decisions? A more profound question is
whether such ones do not hinder the
development of something which immigrants have always tried to put down in
their children, that of calling attention to
their foreign origins".

IN OLDER FAMILIES the offspring were
reprimanded for speaking any other language than English. They were proud of
their new country and wanted their child
ten to "be American."

But multi-language documents, whether they be ballots or something else,
could sap for ever back to the lowering
of hyphenated Americans. This surely fits
with the integration goals which have
been of great concern to government in
the past couple of decades.

IF THE PRINCIPLES of the philosophy of the law are not to be of concern,
certainly the excessive costs in dollars
and forest resources merit another look
at what has been done.

Were it not for those factors one might
view the printing of the ballots in Spanish
with more favor. If it hadn't been for
the gold rush all Californians might still
be Mexicans.

Earl G. Waters is a Sacramento-based
syndicated writer covering state government.

Good neighbor policy

Home Alert helps
to curb burglaries

Last year 9,000 burglaries were reported to Sacramento police.
That's an impressive statistic, but not too meaningful—unless
you've been one of the victims. Then it's merely a rotten experi-
ence.

Your house is your home, your hearth, your castle. When you
return home and discover it's been violated by burglars you feel
feeling helpless, frustrated, even degraded. The walls have been
breached and there's not a thing you can do about it.

If you're lucky, it doesn't
happen again. If you're not
lucky, it's the start of a
nightmare of break-ins, van-
dalism and harassment such
as was visited on a South
Sacramento neighborhood
three years ago.

JUVENILES AGED 15
TO 18 were blamed for the
wave of lawlessness, or K.
V. Lee reported in a har-
rowing account of one
neighborhood's trial by terror. On the rare occasions that they were
apprehended, our juvenile system promptly released them to par-
ents, some of whom showed no inclination to curb their misbehav-
ior.

But police report that the crime crisis in this neighborhood
recently has tapered off, and much of the credit must go to the
police department's increasingly effective Home Alert program to
enlist householders in the everyday battle against neighborhood
crime.

So far, residents of 71 Sacramento city neighborhoods have
joined in Home Alert, a program designed to tighten their own home
security and to look out for their neighbor in the best tradition of
early-day settlers.

MANY MORE SUCH PROGRAMS are needed to add eyes and
ears to over-worked police patrol units. As in any reform move-
ment all it takes is some ardor or woman to take the lead to try
"enough." All that person must do is persuade a half dozen or more
neighbors to agree to come to a series of Home Alert meetings in
the home to hear police experts lecture on crime prevention.

The County Sheriff's office has a similar, but smaller unit.
However, if the County Supervisors are favorably Monday on a
request for $10,134 in matching funds. Sheriff Duane Lowe's depart-
ment will receive a federal-state grant that will provide funds for
five deputy sheriffs and a stenographer and equipment to launch a
full-scale Home Alert program.

City police crime prevention officers emphasize to residents
that to be successful, a burglar must have the desire, the ability,
and the opportunity. Homeowners are told how they can deprive the
lawbreaker the ability and opportunity.

At three home meetings, residents learn how they can borrow
an engraver from a public library branch or the police department
so they can inscribe a driver's license number on personal property.
Decals are provided for house windows to alert would-be thieves
that property is marked so it can be identified.

OFFICERS ADVISE on how best to lock doors and windows,
how to guard against child molesters, crime on the streets and bunco
artists. They offer tips on suspicious behavior by persons in the
neighborhood, such as juveniles loitering during school hours, juven-
iles heading into the backyard of a neighbor known to be wary, or
possibly acting as a lookout in front of a home, or slowly cruising
autos. Officers caution residents not to try to apprehend a suspi-
cious person, but to call police.

A positive spin-off to these Home Alert meetings has been a
renewed feeling of neighborliness. Coffee, cookies and a mutual
concern for the well-being of their neighborhood are the ingredients
of a reawakened spirit of caring that is too often forgotten in a met-
ropolitan neighborhood.

For the resident of a crime-ridden neighborhood who cries
"enough," the city police telephone number to call is 449-5731.

A name that could live in infamy
Child molester Rodriguez faces sentencing again

By K. W. LEE
Staff Writer

The name of Rudolfo A. Rodriguez,
convicted three times of child molesting,
could well come back to haunt the Califor-
nia Supreme Court, which in recent years
has been decreeing how offenders should
be released from prison.

Last summer, the high court ordered
the California Adult Authority to release
Rodriguez from prolonged imprisonment
on his second child molesting offense
under a one-year to life prison term.

The adult authority had warned the jus-
tices that it couldn't predict the behavior
of the sexually disturbed man, a former
mental hospital patient.

Shortly after his release, the 50-year-
old man was back in custody for his third
sexual offense, molesting a 5-year-old girl
in a kindergarten bathroom in Santa Mon-
ica. He was later convicted by a jury of
the same one-year to life offense.

This week Superior Court Judge Ray-
mond Choate, receiving two psychiatric

reports, ruled that Rodriguez is "a men-
tally disordered sex offender" and not
amenable to treatment in state hospital.

Rodriguez faces sentencing April 16.

The Supreme Court's so-called Rodri-
guez decision virtually replaced the
state's indeterminate sentencing system by
directing the authority (the parole board)
to fix "prime terms" for inmates within a
reasonable time after they begin serving
sentences.

The ruling caused release of hundreds
of inmates who had served longer prison
terms because of extraordinary mental
and other behavior problems. Its impact
alarmed not only law enforcement agen-
cies but local parole agents who com-
plained that the decision has spawned
"walking time bombs" among the newly
released inmates without adequate men-
tal and medical programs outside to take
care of them.

After Rodriguez was arrested last fall,
the parole board's spokesman protested
that the justices could have merely
ordered the board to place the man under

state hospital care.

Deputy Dist. Atty. Malcolm Jordan,
who prosecuted the Santa Monica case,

attacked the high court ruling, charging
that the justices had released thousands
of prisoners just to prove a point in law
and that the victims were the real losers
in the case. Nobody knows how many oth-
er Californians have been victimized
ironically. If Rodriguez is sent to prison
on again, his term will be fixed by the
parole board under the specific Rodri-
guez guidelines set by the high court.

And the Rodriguez appeals his fixed
term, the court will eventually act on his
appeal again.

Under the Rodriguez landmark deci-
sion, each inmate has the right to have
his primary term fixed as soon as possible
and his appeal to the higher courts on
the grounds that it is cruel and unusual
punishment.

A three-member minority, led by Associ-
ate Justice Frank Richardson, dissented from the four-member majority rul-
ing, saying the Legislature is better
equipped than the court to develop the
procedural changes in the penal system
mandated by the court's majority.

Senator Davis, born in Winchester,
Virginia, migrated to California by way of
Ohio and Missouri, where he had been
educated in engineering, land surveying
and law, and a News Editor. During the
Mexican War, he raised a company of
infantry volunteers. Commissioned

He migrated to Sacramento in 1850;
engaged as a Dairyman. He was State
Senator 1852-1853; Governor Bigler
named him to command a supply train
for the relief of overland immigrants.

Edward Gilbert, Editor of the San
Francisco Daily Alta California, bitterly
opposed the project which resulted in a
Duel with Denver; Gilbert took second
place in the contest; he was killed August
2, 1852. Public opinion was with Denver
and no action was taken against him.

During 1853, Governor Bigler appointed Denver Secretary of State. He was
elected to Congress September 6, 1854.
In 1857, President Buchanan appointed
Denver Commissioner of Indian Affairs;
May 1858, President Buchanan named
Denver as Governor of Kansas Territory.
The City of Denver, Colorado was named

after him; 1861 President Lincoln commissioned him a Brigadier General;
Denver served a useful and important life
and died August 9, 1892. Denver was 6
foot 2 inches; he was described as "genial,
dignified and urbane."

REPLY

Dear Henry: Many thanks for your letter dated October 22, 1975. You have given us a good idea. When I mail out the yearly dues statement I will include a report on how the Association's money is being used. Please go over the attached statement on it. EX-LEGISLATOR NOW RUNS LAW PRACTICE IN MIDDLE OF NOWHERE
BY CHARLES HILLINGER
Times Staff Writer

NEWBERRY SPRINGS, Calif. — Mounted on a pole on a dirt
road in the desert hangs the sign: "Stanford Shaw, Lawyer."
Nailed to a fence post beneath the sign is a weathered wooden
directional arrow, also with the name Shaw.
There's not a house or a building in sight. Only tumbleweeds,
endless sand, dunes and bone dry mountains on the horizon.
A most unlikely spot for a practicing barrister.
Shaw's sprawling ranch-house and law office are a mile down
the dirt road on the banks of the Mojave River.
"I get quite a range of reactions from people on their first visit
out here," admitted the 62-year-old attorney. "Some get lost trying to find me or get bogged down
in sand. Some say: 'Jesus Christ!' Others, 'Wow! What a place.'"
Many of Shaw's clients knew him when his office was in the
city, in San Bernardino, 100 miles southwest of Newberry
Springs.
Others come to him because of his reputation.
"Some people looking for an attorney pick my name out of
the 'Yellow Pages,'" he explained.
"They call my number, which is a San Bernardino exchange,
having no idea that I'm stuck way out in the middle of the
desert."
Shaw bought 68 acres and moved to the desert 12 years ago to
recover from ill health. He had been San Bernardino County's
state senator and a former member of the Assembly.

"What really throws a lot of people is this part of the desert,"
continued the attorney as he opened the sliding door of his office
and walked a few feet to the lake in his backyard.
Newberry Springs is a 12-mile-wide, 1-2-mile-long valley with
about 1,000 persons living on scattered ranches. Many are poultry
farmers, some are retired.
"To look around," said Shaw, "you'd think this was typical
desert wasteland. But the valley sits on an enormous underground water supply that goes back to glacial times."
Shaw said about 10 years ago a subterranean lake in Newberry
Springs sunk a well, hired earthmovers to bulldoze the land behind his lot and created an artificial lake.
"Now, practically everybody living in the valley has a lake."
Not only is there the historic underground water, but the
Mojave River passes through the valley. Except that the river
itself is dry because the water flows beneath the ground.
"When we drill people we live on a river and have a
lake in our backyard they think we're giving them a line," said
Shaw. "But we're not."
If you describe Newberry Springs to a stranger after hearing all
this, it is easy to see why there would be a certain amount of
disbelief.
An attorney whose office is on a dirt road in the middle of
nowhere, 200 lakes and a dry river. . . .

One would be better much less time and effort spent upon in interim committee study of problems and proposals. The other would abolish the "dog eat" authorship method and require all proposals on the same subject to be consolidated into one proposal through the drafting of a single bill.

Such a procedure would from another gloss producing voluminous law that of the administrative regulations.

Expansion of the authority of administrative agencies has and only created a new problem namely the need for adequate restraints upon the excesses of administrative agencies. Amen 'brother'

embedding.qa_text: May 13, 1976 Mr. Don A. Allen, Sr. Executive Secretary-Treasurer Association of Former California Legislators 837 Commons Drive Sacramento CA 95825 Dear Mr. Allen: Thank you for your letter of May 3, 1976, seeking advice with respect to the homecoming event of the Association of Former California Legislators. In a telephone conversation explaining your letter, you informed me that included among the former legislators who are eligible to attend the event are individuals who are presently elected state officials (for example, Lt. Gov. Dymally, Sec'y of State Eu, Treasurer Unruh). The prohibition against gifts from lobbyists applies to gifts to elected state officials. Accordingly, although the above mentioned individuals are no longer legislators, they nevertheless still are individuals to whom lobbyists cannot make gifts of $10 or more in a calendar month. To avoid any problems under the Political Reform Act, you should ask that these individuals pay for their own meals and drinks instead of having a lobbyist pay for these items. Of course, with respect to former legislators who are not state candidates, elected state officials, legislative officials or agency officials, no problem is created by having a lobbyist pay for the costs of the event. If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to contact me. Very truly yours, [signature] Kenneth Finney Chief Legal Division KF:pvp The image you provided is completely blank, with no visible text, numbers, or any other content. It appears to be an empty page or a page with no content added. If you have another page or section of the document that you would like me to review, please provide it. May 3, 1976 Mr. Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman Fair Political Practice Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Sir: In accord with telephone inquiry made to Executive Director Michael Bennett, and his advice, I respectfully submit the following question. Is there any prohibition or restriction on a lobbyist making a contribution toward the Homecoming events of the Association of Former California Legislators? The event will take place in Sacramento June 10th and 11th, 1976. Our association is a historian, social and commemorative group of former California legislators. We are non-partisan - no longer political in the sense that we do not endorse candidates or issues. We have never caused to be introduced or advocated bills or measures to the legislature, nor will we ever do so. I enclose two of our quarterly bulletins so that you may judge the function of our association. We ask this question for clarification because prior to Proposition 9, many members of the Third House wanted to participate with us in the renewal of old friendships. To that end, they would defray the costs of some meals and cocktails at an overall equally shared average of $600.00 per Homecoming. Many of the Third House members would like to continue this tradition. We, and they, want to operate within the law. Thank you for your consideration and advice. Semper Vaya con Dios, Don A.
